data.title: BUXX: Active ETF With 4.9% Yield, Low Credit Risk And Low Duration
data.description:
This article updates my review of August 2025 in light of current portfolio metrics and recent performance.

BUXX strategy

Strive Enhanced Income Short Maturity ETF (BUXX) is an actively managed fund launched on 08/09/2023 with an expense ratio of 0.26%. BUXX has a 30-day SEC yield of 4.45%, a trailing 12-month yield of 4.91%, a yield-to-worst of 5.06% and pays monthly distributions. As described in the prospectus by Strive Assets Management, the fund invests in U.S. dollar-denominated investment-grade debt securities and seeks to maintain a weighted average maturity of less than two years and a weighted average duration of less than one year.

BUXX primarily invests in residential mortgage-backed securities (“RMBS”), asset-backed securities (“ABS”), collateralized loan, debt and mortgage obligations (“CLOs”, “CDOs”, “CMOs”), corporate debt, U.S. treasuries and government agency securities, and may also invest in funds and BDCs. The fund typically invests more than 25% of its assets in mortgage related instruments, less than 25% in CLOs, and avoid concentration in any other debt sectors. It may also borrow up to one third of its total assets and use derivatives for investing or hedging purposes. Securities are selected and weighted based on market conditions, fundamentals, technicals, interest rates, macroeconomic analysis and liquidity. The turnover rate was 54% in the most recent fiscal year and 83% in the previous year.

Portfolio

The portfolio holds 329 securities. U.S. issuers represent 90.5% of asset value, and investment vehicles structured in the Caiman Islands weigh 7.6%, based on Fidelity data. About 30% of asset value is in securities with an “AAA” S&P rating and 69.7% in securities of lower investment grade.

BUXX has low interest rate risk, with an effective duration of 0.75 years. It means that for every 1% change in interest rates, the price is expected to change by approximately 0.75% in the opposite direction.

The portfolio is focused on securitized debt instruments (64.2%) and also has significant weight in corporate bonds (25.4%). Treasuries represent less than 3% of assets.

Strive Enhanced Income Short Maturity ETF has a 5% yield from short term debt investment grade securities. BUXX has a focus on securitized debt, in particular mortgage related instruments and collateralized obligations. BUXX is in the average compared to its peers based on yield, return and risk. Collateralized loan obligations ETFs are a compelling alternative in investment-grade debt. data.title: BBCA Versus SPY: For Canada, Things Will Get Worse Before They Get Better
data.description:
I like Canada. I believe they’re one of our greatest allies. Growing up, I could see Canada from my window. For my 19th birthday, Canada was my place to go (so I could legally enjoy a few drinks.) Essentially, Canada is a country I have a soft spot for and I do not agree with the Trump Administration putting tariffs on them. Yes, there is a massive trade imbalance, but that trade imbalance actually favors the United States (more on that in a moment).

Unfortunately, conditions for Canada are likely to get worse before they get better, and as a result, I must rate JPMorgan BetaBuilders Canada ETF (BBCA) a sell for now. This is one of those cases where I wish I could justify an investment, but I simply can’t. Should tensions with Canada ease and if a trade deal looks reachable, I would likely upgrade my rating to a hold at the very least. For now, however, it seems that the global trade war will continue.

The United States, on the other hand, remains strong overall. While I am seeing some things that worry me regarding the American economy, I don’t feel a need to exit the market. As such, the State Street SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY) remains a buy in my book. That said, I do advise investors to monitor economic indicators and prepare defensive strategies.

The Illusionary Trade Deficit

Few countries have been hit as hard by the Trump Administration as Canada. Various parties within the Administration have alleged that Canada has been ripping the United States off. Yet, as I’ve explored elsewhere, America’s trade deficit with Canada is all too often misrepresented. We can’t compare Canada head-to-head with the United States because America’s population is about 9 times larger than Canada's. Expecting a country of ~42 million to consume as much or more than a country home to ~340 million is patently unfair and perhaps a bit ludicrous.

Instead, we should break down the amount of American goods Canadians buy on a per capita basis. Canada imported roughly $314.5 billion in goods from the United States in 2025. Broken down per capita, this amounts to nearly $9,000 worth of American goods purchased per Canadian. Meanwhile, the United States purchased $383 billion worth of Canadian goods, which breaks down to about $1,120 per American. The U.S. enjoys a services surplus and sent ~$90 billion worth of services to Canada in 2024, up 4.5% ($3.9 billion) from 2023. U.S. services imports from Canada in 2024 were a mere $57.0 billion, up 3.6% ($2.0 billion) from 2023.

Add it all up and Canada is spending far more on American goods and services on a per capita basis. If anyone has a right to complain about the trade deficits, Canada has the stronger argument, in my opinion. Ultimately, the United States enjoys the stronger position in the trade relationship, bolstering American markets, including SPY.

Canada’s Economy Continues To Deteriorate

Unfortunately, Canada is on recession watch because the economy has contracted in two of the last three quarters, including the last quarter in 2025. A technical recession occurs when an economy contracts for two quarters in a row. Should the economy contract in the current quarter, Canada would end up in a technical recession.

As illustrated above, Canada’s unemployment rate is quite high, 6.7% in 2025. While unemployment has cooled a bit in recent weeks, it’s still elevated from a historical perspective. Further, Canada has shed over 100,000 full-time jobs so far in 2026. Consumer spending may be weakening as well, with Canadian consumers increasingly turning to private labels and emphasizing value. (We are seeing similar trends in the U.S., for the record.)

The United States economy, meanwhile, remains strong, at least as far as the headline numbers go. The unemployment rate did tick up to 4.4% in February, from 4.3% in January, but this is still well below Canada’s unemployment rate. American exports to Canada weigh in at 15.14% share of all American exports, the first time that this number has dropped below 16% in quite some time. This has been fueled by Canadian consumer boycotts. Further, Canadian travelers to the United States plummeted by more than 20% to 4.2 million. American visitors to Canada also declined, although only by 7.5%, good for 1.6 million visits. Given how massive the American population is compared to Canada, it’s rather surprising that the gap between the two is so high.

Unfortunately, I believe Canada’s economy will continue to struggle unless it can ink an equitable trade deal with the United States. Canada has been trying to diversify away from dependence on the United States, but this is far easier said than done. America is facing headwinds as well, but despite the turmoil, the United States still remains one of the safer havens to park wealth in.

I do worry about the American dollar and its slide versus other currencies. However, in the U.S. versus Canada exchange rate, USD vs CAD, the Canadian dollar has been losing ground while the American dollar has been rising in value. This is likely due to the poor economic environment in Canada. While I believe international diversification is smart, I don’t believe Canada offers good diversification at this time. Canada’s fate remains tied to the United States, thus reducing diversification potential.

BBCA Fundamentals And Valuation Considerations

One of the things I noticed right off the bat is the low adjusted expense ratio, which is a mere 0.19%. I’ve looked at a variety of foreign market ETFs and rates of 0.5% or greater are common. This is one of the cheaper ETFs I’ve seen. That said, BBCA is more expensive than SPY’s 0.095%. Where BBCA really excels compared to SPY is in the PE department. Morningstar data notes that BBCA has a portfolio-wide PE ratio of 17.83, compared to SPY’s 22.36. It should be noted that key European market ETFs, like Italy, often have PE ratios below 15. BBCA also wins in the PS ratio at 2.52 to SPY’s 3.20.

Unfortunately, the weak economic environment and tough negotiations with the Trump Administration increase risks substantially. This alone should result in lower PE ratios, and in this situation, I’d argue that the PE and PS ratios for BBCA are too high given the headwinds. SPY may not seem like a better deal, but I think it has comparable upside with substantially lower risks.

BBCA has a hefty amount of portfolio weight in financials. This is pretty common for foreign ETFs, and by and large, this lowers my preferences for many of them. I’d prefer to see higher diversification. SPY tracks the S&P 500, and owing to the vast valuations of leading tech companies, it’s heavily concentrated in tech. As such, from a diversification perspective, I believe investors need to look beyond SPY, adding other ETFs not as heavily concentrated in technology. In both cases, the top holding in both portfolios weighs in at about 7.5%, and the top 10 stocks make up ~36% for SPY and 42% for BBCA, suggesting a solid degree overall of diversification

As for dividends, BBCA offers a decent 1.77% TTM yield and a 5-year CAGR north of 5%. SPY offers just a ~1% dividend yield along with a ~5% 5-year CAGR. While I wouldn’t rely on either as a major income investment, BBCA’s ~2% yield is reasonably attractive, especially given the ETF’s low costs.

The 5-year tracking error for BBCA is a mere 1.85%, which is pretty good in my book, especially for foreign market ETFs. Many such ETFs have double-digit tracking errors. Of course, SPY is a gold standard as far as tracking errors are concerned, with a 5-year error rate of just 0.07%. As for ratings, BBCA gets high marks, as shown below. I agree with these grades in and of themselves. BBCA on paper is attractive. However, the larger macroeconomic issues, which can be difficult to catch with data, keep me at a sell rating. SPY also gets mostly good marks, although risk gets a B-, which I personally feel is too low a grade.

BBCA

SPY

Why I Could Be Wrong

President Trump changes his mind rather frequently. While it appears that both sides are digging in, Trump could simply change his mind, especially with inflation heating up and the war in Iran likely to add to American financial deficits and inflation. With midterms fast approaching, Trump may decide to pause or end tariffs, likely hoping that doing so will cool inflation. Further, given that 51% of Americans oppose tariffs on Canada, Trump may decide to shore up voters by dropping the tariffs.

SPY remains a buy for right now, meaning I don’t believe investors need to exit the market. However, I do feel like the economy has formed a sort of house of cards, and a major event could quickly spark a sustained and deep sell-off. If so, hopefully I catch wind before everything is set in motion.

Key Takeaway: Now Isn’t The Time To Go Canadian

I hope that Canada comes out of negotiations with the Trump Administration with a good deal. That said, we can’t be sure that that will happen and there’s a high chance that President Trump will continue to stir the pot. Still, the approaching midterms provide at least some hope that President Trump will ease up. On the whole, however, Canada and thus BBCA face too strong of headwinds for me to invest right now. SPY remains a buy, but investors should closely monitor the economy for signs of trouble.

data.title: Diana Shipping: Proposed Genco Acquisition Unlikely To Benefit Shares - Hold
data.description:
Note:

I have covered Diana Shipping Inc. or "Diana Shipping" (DSX, DSX.PR.B, DSX.WS) previously, so investors should view this as an update to my earlier articles on the company.

Please note also that Diana Shipping, Genco Shipping & Trading (GNK) and Star Bulk Carriers (SBLK) are part of our coverage universe at Value Investor's Edge.

Reflecting On A Bad Call

Eighteen months ago, I upgraded shares of leading dry bulk shipper Diana Shipping from "Hold" to "Buy" due to a number of factors:

  • improvements in the dry bulk shipping market.
  • deeply discounted valuation.
  • expectations for the company's generous quarterly dividend to remain safe for the time being.

Unfortunately, this turned out to be disastrous call, as the company reduced the quarterly dividend by 87% just two months after my article was published. While this was partially offset by a generous self-tender offer, mediocre market conditions and the Trump Tariffs shock resulted in the stock price dropping by more than 40% until April 2025.

While shares have recovered all losses in recent quarters, Diana Shipping has underperformed peers quite meaningfully:

Disappointing Fourth Quarter Results

Last month, the company reported another set of disappointing quarterly results with both revenues and profitability falling short of expectations.

While Diana Shipping's conservative chartering approach results in rather stable average daily time charter equivalent ("TCE") rates, increased operating expenses and the sale of some older vessels have negatively impacted core earnings and cash flows in recent quarters.

In fact, fourth quarter operating cash flow of $7.3 million represented a new multi-year low for the company. Diana Shipping ended the quarter with $122.2 million in cash, cash equivalents and liquid investments as well as $636.1 million in debt.

Proposed Acquisition Of Genco Shipping & Trading

In recent quarters, the company has built a sizeable position in shares of larger competitor Genco Shipping & Trading or "Genco Shipping." According to regulatory filings, Diana Shipping has spent $103.5 million to accumulate a 14.8% stake in Genco Shipping.

Four months ago, the company announced a proposal to acquire the remaining shares for $20.60 in cash which was rejected by Genco Shipping's board of directors.

In response, Diana Shipping launched a proxy fight by nominating six director candidates for election to Genco Shipping's board of directors.

Two weeks ago, the company increased its offer to $23.50 per share with $1.43 billion in committed financing arranged by DNB Carnegie and Nordea with participation from leading international banks, including DNB, Nordea, BNP Paribas, Standard Chartered, Deutsche Bank and Danske Bank.

In addition, market leader Star Bulk Carriers has entered into a definitive agreement with Diana Shipping to acquire 16 Genco vessels for $470.5 million in cash upon completion of the proposed transaction.

Together, the fully committed financing and the definitive agreement with Star Bulk provide a clear and executable path to complete the acquisition of Genco’s outstanding shares, refinance existing indebtedness, and pay related transaction expenses.

The committed financing is fully underwritten and not conditioned on completion of the Star Bulk transaction.

On Thursday, Genco Shipping's board of directors rejected the revised proposal due to, among other things,

  • the lack of an appropriate premium to net asset value ("NAV").
  • a $0.32 billion disparity between the amount of committed financing stated in Diana Shipping's press release and the actual commitment letter filed with the SEC.
  • the proposed "fire sale" of 16 vessels to Star Bulk Carriers for an aggregate price of $470.5 million.

While Genco Shipping's objections carry some merit, seeking a premium to NAV may be perceived as overly ambitious given that its primary peers trade at significant discounts:

In addition, Diana Shipping's agreement to sell 16 vessels at an estimated 10-15% discount to Star Bulk Carriers should not be a cause for concern for Genco Shipping's board of directors as long as they manage to negotiate the best possible price for the company's equity holders.

Once a transaction has been agreed on and approved by shareholders, it doesn't really matter anymore what exactly Diana Shipping does with the acquired vessels, at least not for Genco Shipping's shareholders.

On Friday, Diana Shipping commented on the rejection of the revised proposal by Genco Shipping's board of directors. In addition to the issues discussed by me above, the company clarified that $331 million in additional committed financing are related to a partial refinancing of Diana Shipping's existing debt.

Genco’s actions lead us to conclude that this Board and management team are more focused on entrenching themselves than maximizing value for their shareholders.

We, therefore, have no choice but to proceed with our effort to elect to the Genco Board independent directors who will act in the best interest of all shareholders by exploring all meaningful opportunities for value creation.

In my opinion, Genco Shipping's board of directors should engage with Diana Shipping and negotiate a transaction price closer to the company's estimated net asset value of approximately $25 per share.

The combined company would become the second largest U.S. exchange-traded dry bulk shipping pure play with an on-the-water fleet of 66 vessels.

In addition, Genco Shipping's lower leverage would result in a stronger balance sheet for the enlarged Diana Shipping.

However, without changes to the company's overly conservative chartering strategy and a return to more generous dividend payments, I do not expect Diana Shipping's NAV discount to decrease meaningfully.

For my part, I would rather consider an investment in industry leader Star Bulk Carriers which is also part of our Income Model Portfolio at Value Investor's Edge.

Assuming Diana Shipping manages to acquire Genco Shipping and second hand vessel values remaining stable, Star Bulk Carriers would increase its fleet size by more than 10% at a material discount to estimated net asset value.

Please note also that Star Bulk Carriers expects to distribute 100% of excess cash flow to common shareholders on a quarterly basis. Given solid market conditions, an annual dividend yield of 10% might be in the cards for this year.

As a potential acquisition of Genco Shipping & Trading is not likely to result in substantially increased shareholder capital returns or changes to Diana Shipping's chartering approach, I am downgrading the company's common shares from Buy to Hold.

Bottom Line

Diana Shipping's financial results remain impacted by a combination of decreased fleet size and higher operating expenses as well as the company's overly conservative chartering strategy.

In recent quarters, Diana Shipping has built a substantial stake in larger peer Genco Shipping & Trading and offered to acquire the remainder of the company at a price of $23.50 per share in cash.

As Genco Shipping's board has been reluctant to engage with Diana Shipping, a proxy fight appears inevitable.

However, even if Diana Shipping manages to acquire its larger competitor, the transaction is not likely to result in substantially increased shareholder capital returns or changes to Diana Shipping's overly conservative chartering approach.

Consequently, I am downgrading the company's common shares from Buy to Hold.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: UPM-Kymmene: Upside Is Narrowing In 2026
data.description:
UPM Kymmene (UPMMY) has been on my list of companies for investment for quite a while. My investment stake was allocated between 2024 and 2025, mostly during mid 2025. It's now one of the largest stakes in my personal and commercial portfolio. There are key reasons why I believe the company is highly likely to outperform both its peers and the market over the next few years, and why I am fine holding it for the time until that price and valuation is achieved. I'll explain to you in this article why I in fact believe that the upside is unlikely to materialize in 2026, but why I think 2027 and 2028E are likely years for further upside.

I have a previous article on the company, and you can in fact view my previous coverage here, including a rather fortuitous targeting in October/November of 2025, where I managed to capture a "low" for a relatively significant buy - one of the larger ones before I mostly stopped buying, which has outperformed the SPY by a significant margin.

I have likened investing and the way I view my portfolio to a few things over the past few years. Today I want to draw a likeness to a tree or a garden. You set your seeds/watch your tree. You watch it grow, develop. You prune where necessary, and you harvest the parts of the plants with fruitful yields. You plant new ones. The garden metaphor is probably better than the tree, since sometimes you have to "burn" parts down, or admit defeat and clear a portion (though this, for me, has been rare).

In this metaphor of the garden, UPM Kymmene is a large patch of plants that grows slowly - but surely. The past 6 months have seen impressive performance - but it's not strange when you see what's been happening both in the broader timber and correlated markets, as well as in the company itself.

Today I will both provide an update as well as cover my reasons for having this position, and my target for harvesting.

UPM Kymmene - Upside is there, but it's lower than before

I meant to write this article a few weeks ago, when the company traded above €27.4/share. At that time, the truth of the matter was that the company was below 20% annualized, even with yield, and I meant to tell you that it's really worse than it once was. Given that we're now back down to €25/share, it's not as bad as all that, as you'll see in the valuation. The upside is still there - it's just that compared to my investment cost basis at close to €23, it's not close to 30% annualized anymore.

What makes UPM Kymmene, a Finnish, BBB-rated forest player, so great is both its mix of operations, its move from traditional paper to sustainable materials and to biofuel, added to by a substantial forest reserve and power generation in terms of assets. It creates, in my view, the perfect play on European forestry. There is, to my mind, no better positioned, no better capitalized, and no company with a much better future or upside in this sector, at the right price. It's one of the highest-conviction investments I have been able to make as of late.

The expansions into Uruguay and Germany have only added to the international characteristics of the company, and the aforementioned vertical integration is a prime factor and driver for investment. We have the segments fibres, adhesive materials (Previously called Raflatac), specialty papers, energy, and Biorefining. Yes, that's right - this company, aside from being a major forest player, is also one of the largest electricity producers in all of Finland. They have this not only through major hydro plans along several rivers, but also a significant share in the Olkiluoto 3 plant (nuclear).

In the last annuals, 2024-2025, the company's sales were mostly related to renewable fibers, advanced materials, and its decarbon and ESG solutions. It's therefore fair to say that UPM Kymmene is a company that has managed to bridge the ESG gap.

The recent 2025 results confirm this in full.

The backdrop here was a very challenging operating environment. Despite this operating environment, the company saw a full-year CFO of €1.4B, and an intensification of its transformation. Biofuels was superb (and is likely, given Hormuz, to see further interest) and managed a positive EBIT contribution. The new biochemical plant in Leuna made its first customer delivery, and startups in the advanced materials are growing in APAC. UPM worked on closing down unproductive sites of Ettringen and Kaukas, entered into a JV with Sappi for graphic papers, and acquired Metamark for adhesive materials.

In short, a very solid and busy year. Transformation ongoing, and on track for a strong finish in 2027E - in my view.

Some analysts will, of course, argue that comparable EBIT was down. I say that this is not only acceptable but good, given the substantial cost environment and declining markets in legacy graphic paper and printer paper.

The company also gave us guidance for the coming fiscal year to help us with assumptions and expectations. 1H26 profit is a wide range, between €325M to €525M, which is both above and below the 2025 1H and 2H. Why the wide range?

There's a fair amount of uncertainty related to input cost assumptions for the entire year, worsened now by the Iran war, which has impacted the chemical and energy markets, as well as the ramp-up of new production and weakness in some legacy markets. UPM has a high correlation to pulp and electricity price - a €50/change in pulp prices I has an impact of about €190M, or as much as €250M on the company's EBIT on an annual basis. Electricity, meanwhile, is at €40M for every €10/MWh change in electricity. Then, given the company's operations, there's now significant FX.

So we need to be more comfortable with volatility if we want to own, invest in, or understand this company. Also, it's worth noting that the results in 2h25 were somewhat impacted by national/regional energy refunds - these are not expected to recur in 2026, which means that the 2026E estimate of a 15% AEPS growth after a 23% AEPS decline in 2025 (YoY) might even be considered optimistic.

So why am I so "gung-ho" about this company's future?

Imagine a UPM where the Biochemical plant has reached full potential and is no longer subject to start-up costs, where the JV is completed, the plywood business is reviewed, and exposure to graphic paper markets is completed. Imagine also the continuation of growth in decarbon and advanced materials, as well as a more internationalized UPM (ongoing, South America), as well as the modernization (ongoing) of Tyrvää, which will reduce CO2 by 15% and improve efficiency.

Why is that 15% so significant? Under the EU ETS (the emission trading system), any reduction in CO2 immediately impacts carbon costs that the company needs to pay. I won't bore you with ETS calculations, but generally speaking, reductions mean reduced scrutiny under Article 10c (though not exactly intended for Finland, rather Eastern Europe), lower CoC, modernization funds, aside from the obvious increased cash flow.

These converging upsides are, in my view, the reasons for positivity.

Valuation is as follows.

Valuation for UPM Kymmene - Upside is there

The company's net debt of 2.29x is entirely manageable, and the company has cash and credit of €2.7B available, with no financial covenants. Furthermore, the company used the low valuation of the company to buy back shares. This is an instance where I do not have an issue with a company doing this.

The dividend proposal for 2025 further enhances the view that the company is expecting to see significant upside.

I tend to price UPM at 17-18x P/E, as opposed to 15-16x. This is despite 15-16x P/E still giving market-beating upside at a valuation of €25/share. I give the company a higher P/E because of its premium nature, its mix of assets, and its strategic positioning on the market. I believe firmly that allowing it to trade lower on estimate would be a disservice to the premium that I believe is valid.

I believe the potential recovery from this downcycle in paper, pulp, and timber is likely to generate earnings where €2/share is going to look like a very conservative set of earnings.

Normalizing the company at about 17x P/E brings me to a fair value target, adjusted for a growth rate of about 17-18% per year until 2028E, with an emphasis on 2027E, to about €37/share in fair value. The upside to that would be about 21% here per year, and that's inclusive of the company's near-6% yield. So at 15-16x P/E, we're talking about 15-17% per year, of which 6% is yield. To lose money or to see negative returns on this company, we'd have to see a persistent and longer downturn or some sort of operational or strategic mistake on the part of the company.

While based on the company's forecast accuracy (about 35% negative failure) this is not an impossibility, I believe UPM is well-positioned and well constructed for the challenges that are ahead. Not only are they very much ahead of Scandinavian peers, but they are also ahead of other players in the industry.

I am narrowing my price target at this time and applying more conservative valuation assumptions. I am giving the company a €29/share PT with a €37/share, assuming 6% yield, where a 15% annualized rate of return is possible. The company still could go to about €40/share (in fact, in a virtuous cycle, I consider it likely), but the current assumptions and macro call for more of a discount.

Risks & Considerations

I would argue that the primary risk here is actually the volatility and cyclicality of the underlying end markets that UPM works. The company's exposure to biofuels and biochemicals remains a core growth aspect that has yet to pay off, and the company confirms this in its latest earnings release as well. The expectation is for this to pay off in 2027E. And energy is as volatile as ever, especially when some of it is renewable - so we'll see how this ends up (but given today's macro, I believe positive).

Both in terms of peers and in terms of historical upside, as well as the current market conditions for the 3-5 year period, UPM is attractively priced and positioned, which lends itself well to investing for the medium to longer term.

The ADR for UPM is UPMMY, which is a 1/1 ADR, meaning my PT of €29/share comes to about $33.5/share.

My thesis for the company, updated for 4Q25, is as follows.

Thesis

UPM is one of the world's best and most appealing Finnish-based forestry and pulp/chemical companies with a good energy arm. It also has a great yield of over 6% at this particular time. My position in the company is now very significant, and I'm not scared of buying more either.

I'm as of 4Q25, reiterating my rating on the company as a "BUY" here, with a price target of at or around €29/share with a fair value of about €37/share, down from my previous assumption of low €40's in fair value. I no longer view the company as cheap above €24/share.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 4/5 "stars" here, which is enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this and a price target of €29/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Buy" rating as of March 2026.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Tower Semiconductor: Buy The Story, Respect The Risks
data.description:
Introduction

Tower Semiconductor Ltd. (TSEM) is an Israeli-based, Nasdaq-listed specialty semiconductor foundry that has flown under the radar for years but is now starting to attract more investor attention as the market looks for second-order beneficiaries of the AI infrastructure buildout. Unlike the companies designing the most advanced AI processors, Tower operates in specialty analog and mixed-signal manufacturing, which gives it exposure to parts of the semiconductor value chain that are becoming more important as data centers scale and connectivity demands rise.

Tower is active in specialty areas such as silicon photonics, silicon germanium, RF CMOS, BiCMOS, power management, and sensors. Its most relevant products today are SiPho and SiGe, which are used in optical and high-speed connectivity applications.

Since 2005, Tower has expanded revenue substantially despite periodic cyclical setbacks in years such as 2013, 2019, and 2023, which are masked by the five-year reporting period shown in the diagram below. However, the long-term revenue trend remains intact, though only growing at a CAGR of around 5%. The company is profitable, having netted $220M in 2025.

I believe TSEM’s opportunity lies in its position as a supplier of SiPho and SiGe platforms for 1.6T and eventually higher-speed optical modules, a part of the AI buildout that is becoming more important as larger GPU clusters require faster and more power-efficient data transmission, while the company is simultaneously scaling capacity into demand that is already being reserved years ahead.

AI bottleneck

The AI buildout is creating a second bottleneck beyond compute. As GPU clusters get larger, much more data has to move between servers, racks, and switches, which increases the importance of optical connectivity. That is becoming a bigger issue because traditional electrical links consume too much power and lose efficiency at higher speeds. Nvidia (NVDA) has highlighted that co-packaged optical designs can improve power efficiency by up to 3.5X and network resiliency by 10X.

TSEM is positioned in technologies that help move data fast enough for these systems to scale. The company’s most important products for this theme are silicon photonics, or SiPho, and silicon germanium, or SiGe. Tower says its silicon photonics platform is already a leading solution in 400G to 800G optical markets, has entered production for 1.6T products, and is being developed further for 3.2T and co-packaged optics, all of which are directly tied to faster data center connectivity.

Dell’Oro said optical transport equipment grew 10% in 2025, while direct cloud provider purchases grew around 50% as hyperscalers spent more on AI-driven data center interconnect. TSEM's position in this market also looks strong. In a 2026 release with Nvidia, Tower said its silicon photonics platform enables up to double the data rate of prior SiPho solutions for 1.6T modules. Management said in February that Tower is the primary provider for the high adoption of SiPho in 1.6T transceivers, and the company has now raised total planned SiPho and SiGe investment to $920M, targeting capacity above 5x the Q4 2025 shipment run rate, with more than 70% of that capacity already reserved or in reservation through 2028.

The financials are already starting to improve for TSEM, as a look at their income statement shows. Revenue growth is accelerating on a quarterly basis, both in absolute and in relative terms. In Q2, QoQ revenue growth was 3.9%, and in Q4 it already stands at 11.3%.

Profits are starting to grow at a faster rate than revenue, with gross profit up 26.4%, operating profit up 40%, and net profit growing 49.4%, all QoQ.

Taken together, this is a powerful trend that, if continued, can put fundamentals on a very exciting trajectory, which can be very profitable for investors if it is not priced in yet. That brings us to valuation.

Valuation

The P/E ratio has started to adjust, as it shot up to 97, up from trading in the 20s and 10s in the previous years. This reflects that some of the future growth is already priced in. Margins are not constant for this stock, so it is hard to perform a TTM multiple analysis on any type of profitability metric. Their P/S ratio sits around 12, which is significantly elevated historically, but not surprising when considering the thesis exclusively relies on future developments.

Therefore, it makes more sense to value Tower Semiconductor using a ramp scenario and projecting future revenues.

Their own financial model incorporating their $920M planned capacity buildouts, assuming 85% utilization, foresees a 1.8X increase in revenue, reaching $2.84B, and a 3.4X increase in net income, growing to $750M. This gives them a modeled 2028 P/E ratio of 25, which does not look as bad anymore as the trailing multiples.

Actual net income could end up being substantially higher, as they could increase pricing more than is currently being modeled and have higher utilization, and the above projection excludes revenue and margins from Intel’s Fab11 capacity corridor agreement. I do assume that these projections are conservative and the real 2028 P/E is closer to 20 (suggesting around $920M of net income), potentially even below.

Looking at technicals on the daily chart, it becomes clear that the trend remains intact. TSEM broke out of a combination of price and RSI resistance in mid-March, which can yield explosive gains afterwards. It remains above all three key moving averages (50, 100, and 200). This portrays that this uptrend can keep going, even after a slight pullback today. The RSI, however, is already in overbought territory, indicating there might only be a little room left to continue the rally.

Risks

Alongside an already expanding valuation, this stock has its own unique risks. In my view, the main two risks are a prolonged war in Iran and failed execution.

As TSEM has started to gain more visibility, sharply increased in valuation, and become one of retail investors' favorites, it is likely to be very volatile and trade like a risk asset. These are now severely challenged, as the Strait of Hormuz remains closed, increasing the risk of adverse economic effects from elevated oil prices every day. If economic outlooks worsen and markets correct, risk assets get treated with the least amount of love and can sell off heavily.

Execution risk is real because the thesis now depends heavily on a successful SiPho and SiGe ramp. Tower has raised total planned investment in these areas to $920M and said more than 70% of total SiPho capacity is already reserved or in the process of being reserved through 2028. That is bullish, but it also raises the bar. If installation, qualification, or customer ramps come in slower than expected, the market could reassess the stock quickly given how much of the story now rests on future capacity.

Conclusion

In my view, TSEM could be worth a bet, but most likely only as a swing trade, as I do see the risks mounting at this point. I would advise waiting for a slight pullback, which can happen any day given the Iran war uncertainties. I would suggest using a stop loss but leaving upside optionality and not holding until full capacity is reached but instead until the market starts to price the stock far too extensively, which could be at around a 40 P/E for 2028. More conservative investors should probably skip out on this one.

Rating: Initiate at Buy.

data.title: Jefferies: Credit Concerns Overshadow Discounted Valuation
data.description:
As I’ve covered almost two years ago, Jefferies Financial Group Inc. (JEF) does not have any competitive advantage in the investment banking industry and has a cyclical business. While the operating landscape has been positive in recent years for investment banks, Jefferies has been under pressure lately due to its private credit exposures, leading to a very weak share price performance lately. Since my initial coverage on Jefferies back in May 2024, its shares have declined by more than 13%, when including dividends, and have underperformed the overall stock market (SPY) by a significant margin.

In recent months, Jefferies has performed quite badly and has underperformed its closest peers, as the company had exposure to private credit defaults of First Brands Group and U.K.’s Market Financial Solutions (MFS). While losses from these events may be manageable and are within the bank’s risk tolerance, this raised questions about its risk management and credit due diligence, leading to negative sentiment toward its shares over the past few months.

Jefferies was not the only bank exposed to these defaults, as I’ve covered recently on Wells Fargo (WFC), but due to its business concentration in corporate and investment banking it has been much more penalized than its peers by rising concerns about private credit exposures. As shown in the next graph, Jefferies has clearly underperformed some of its closest peers, including JPMorgan Chase (JPM), Bank of America (BAC), or Morgan Stanley (MS), over the last six months, when the first signs about potential issues in the private credit sector emerged, following the defaults of Tricolor Holdings and First Brands Group.

Jefferies’ exposure to First Brands Group was about $715 million in receivables linked to First Brands, even though this was an indirect exposure through Point Bonita, which is a subsidiary of its asset management segment. Of the total exposure, only about $113 million is directly at risk for Jefferies, while the rest was in funds that invested in invoice factoring between First Brands and major retailers.

Despite that, the bank may eventually have to compensate clients for the losses in those funds, something like what Credit Suisse had to do related to Greensill funds some years ago. Indeed, Western Alliance Bancorporation (WAL) has recently sued Jefferies for not making payment of $126 million related to First Brands Group loans, showing that this issue may affect Jefferies more than what the bank was expecting. This also raises litigation risk as more customers may follow the same footsteps of Western Alliance in the future, which is clearly a negative factor for Jefferies’ investment case.

Regarding its exposure to MFS, Jefferies had about £100 million ($133 million) of exposure when the U.K. lender collapsed, through a secured lending facility. According to Jefferies, it expects to lose about £20 million ($26 million) related to this event, which seems to be manageable. However, investors got concerned about the bank’s lending standards and risk appetite, especially considering this was the second high profile default in just a matter of months tied to private credit exposures. It could signal that Jefferies relaxed credit standards in the recent past to pursue credit growth, which could potentially lead to more credit losses in the future.

This background largely explains why investor sentiment towards its stock has been negative over the past few months, something that is not easy to reverse soon considering that high customer withdrawals regarding credit funds could lead to a potential liquidity squeeze in the private credit sector, not boding well for credit valuations and overall quality ahead.

Despite some recent setbacks and doubts about the bank’s risk management and credit due diligence, Jefferies has reported a somewhat positive performance related to fiscal year (FY) 2025, which ended last November. Its credit losses were not very meaningful, as the bank’s business model is largely related to investment banking and capital markets activities and does not take much credit risk like traditional retail & commercial banks. Nevertheless, Jefferies reported a pre-tax loss of about $22 million in the last quarter of FY 2025 related to First Brands, which was a key reason why its quarterly results were slightly below market expectations.

For the full FY 2025, Jefferies’ revenues amounted to more than $7.3 billion, representing an increase of 4.4% YoY. This growth was supported both by higher revenues in investment banking and capital markets, while asset management reported a decline in revenues. Operating expenses were pressured by the inflationary environment, especially related to staff compensation, explaining why total costs increased by 7.4% YoY to nearly $6.5 billion.

Considering that expenses increased at a higher rate than revenues, the bank’s efficiency deteriorated during last year, not being a positive outcome. Its cost-to-income ratio was 89%, which is a very high level and shows that Jefferies’ efficiency is among the worst in the banking sector.

Given this combination of higher cost than revenue growth, Jefferies’ net income amounted to $630 million during the last fiscal year, declining by 5.7% compared to the previous year. This shows that its business is cyclical and operational leverage can work both ways, as relatively small changes in revenues or costs can have a significant impact on the bank’s bottom line. Regarding its profitability, the return on tangible equity (RoTE) ratio was 10.1% in FY 2025, representing a decline of 70 basis points compared to FY 2024.

This operating performance was not particularly impressive, and I don’t expect it to improve much in the short term, especially considering recent market volatility due to the war in Iran that is leading to higher market volatility and lower new deals, especially in debt capital markets. The bank is expected to announce its Q1 FY 2026 results on March 25, which will be quite important to see its operating momentum in recent months and what the management’s outlook is for the rest of the year.

The market seems to be bullish for FY 2026, expecting revenues around $8.3 billion (+12.7% YoY) and a net income above $900 million (+44% YoY). I think this seems to be quite optimistic and may be revised downwards over the coming weeks, particularly if its first quarter earnings come below expectations or its outlook for the rest of the year is seen as soft by investors.

On the other hand, for income-oriented investors Jefferies is now more appealing than it was in the past, as a lower share price has resulted in a higher dividend yield. Jefferies was yielding about 2.6% when I last covered it, which was not particularly attractive compared to other banking stocks, while its current dividend yield is nowadays around 4.2%. This is above both the banking sector average of around 3% and Jefferies’ own history of about 2.8% over the past five years, making it a better income option than it used to be.

Its current quarterly dividend is $0.40 per share, which has been unchanged over the past five quarters, leading to a dividend payout ratio of 57% based on FY 2025 EPS. This seems to be an acceptable level and makes Jefferies’ dividend sustainable in the short to medium term, but investors should consider that its business is cyclical and earnings can be volatile depending on capital markets cycles, not being therefore a ‘safe’ income play over the long term in my opinion.

Moreover, its recent litigation issues related to First Data Group can impact meaningfully its earnings in case of a negative ruling, potentially impacting the sustainability of its dividend in the future.

Regarding its valuation, Jefferies is currently trading at about 0.75x book value, which is close to the bottom valuation levels over the past five years and much lower than its peak valuation of around 1.6x book value at its peak in December 2024. This clearly shows that Jefferies has de-rated considerably over the last year due to macroeconomic issues (the trade war in April 2025) and more recently due to its issues related to private credit exposures, showing that investor sentiment can be quite volatile toward its shares.

Conclusion

Jefferies has reported a mixed performance over the past few quarters as revenue growth was positive, but the bank failed to control cost growth, impacting negatively its profitability. More recently, credit defaults from First Brands Group and MFS raised some concerns about the bank’s risk management and credit underwriting, with more cases possible in the future in my opinion due to its business concentration in corporate and investment banking.

Given this background, it’s not surprising to see that Jefferies’ share price has been weak lately and its valuation multiple has de-rated to discounted levels compared to other banks. While investors may see this backdrop as a signal of undervaluation, I think investor sentiment is likely to remain negative in the near term as liquidity in the private credit sector is not likely to improve much ahead, potentially leading to further defaults in this space. Jefferies can easily be exposed again to further credit losses due to its business model of high exposure to investment banking and capital markets, which can likely lead to further pressure on its share price.

data.title: Prestige Consumer Healthcare: Adding A Prestigious Brand
data.description:
Shares of Prestige Consumer Healthcare (PBH) have seen a small positive reaction in response to the announcement of the acquisition of Breathe Right, taking place on a day when markets at large are suffering from the unrest in the Middle East.

I understand why the reaction is positive, as the deal looks interesting on its merits, yet it adds some leverage while Prestige has seen no real organic growth over the past decade. With shares having come down meaningfully from last year's levels, overall valuations are not too demanding, yet a non-inspiring operating performance and leverage employed make it difficult to have strong conviction yet.

Other and higher conviction ideas, including the areas of consumer brands and medical applications, can be found at Value In Corporate Events.

Acquiring Breathe Right

Prestige Consumer Healthcare announced a $1.045 billion deal to acquire Breathe Right and other brands from Foundation Consumer Healthcare. The effective purchase price of the deal drops to $900 million if anticipated tax benefits are considered.

With the deal, Prestige will get its hands on the number one brand in the nasal strip category, allowing the company to enter into a new category. Such nasal strips are used to improve sleep wellness, address snoring, improve athletic performance, and provide congestion relief, among others. The brand is the undisputed category leader, holding a 60% market share.

The deal adds about $200 million in sales and $90 million in adjusted EBITDA, suggesting a very impressive 45% margin on that front. The Breathe Right brand makes up two-thirds of these sales, complemented by smaller brands like Dimetapp and Anbesol, active in children's cold relief and oral pain relief, respectively.

The deal comes at a roughly 11 times EBITDA multiple, or 9.5 times if synergies are considered. While net debt will shoot up to 4 times EBITDA, this leverage ratio is seen coming down to 3 times a year later. Accretion to margins as well as earnings per share is seen, as well as addition to long-term organic growth rates.

The accompanying deal presentation reveals that pro forma sales will jump from $1.1 billion to $1.3 billion, with the deal seen as accretive to the tune of $0.25 per share. This marks the 11th deal in the past 16 years for the company.

Pro Forma Implications

In February, Prestige reported its third-quarter results for what is its fiscal year 2026. Revenues were down 4% so far this year to $807 million, with most of the decline attributed to strong comparables and limited ability to supply Clear Eyes during the year. Following this softer momentum, the company called for full-year sales to be down about 3% to $1.10 billion, with adjusted earnings seen at $4.54 per share.

The company is on track to generate about $350 million in EBITDA. Regular net debt of $971 million is reported, with leverage reported just over a billion if financial and operating lease liabilities are reported. Pro forma net debt is seen up to $2.02 billion, excluding tax benefits. With pro forma EBITDA seen up to about $440 million here, I see leverage at just over 4.5 times. The company claims 4.0 times EBITDA, likely because of the tax benefits or potential to deleverage ahead of closing, seen in the third quarter of this calendar year.

With 48 million shares trading in the low $60 range here, the market value of Prestige comes in around $3 billion, for a $4 billion enterprise valuation, ahead of the latest deal. This is equivalent to about 3.6 times sales, which are not growing, and about 11-12 times EBITDA. Based on the effective purchase price, the acquired asset is valued around 4.5 times, which represents a small premium, but given the higher margins, EBITDA multiples are actually coming in a bit lower.

Factoring in $90 million of an EBITDA contribution and assuming a 2-3% depreciation intensity in relation to sales, pre-tax profits could see an $85 million boost. However, a 5% cost of debt on $1.05 billion in additional net debt would result in pre-tax profits of $32 million, or about $24 million after taxes. That calculation reveals about half a dollar accretion to earnings per share, higher than the quarter per share guided for, likely as debt costs come in a bit higher.

What Now?

The reality is that Prestige has been struggling for quite a period of time, and with earnings power seen at just over $4.50 per share, shares trade at 13-14 times adjusted earnings. That is for a non-growing business: in fact, one seeing some small revenue declines, although already operating with quite a bit of net debt, being leveraged about three times.

Leverage will increase quite a bit, but given the reasonable multiples, I understand why shares are up about two dollars, or 3%, in a down market. This adds some $100 million to the value here, supported by strong brands and very reasonable multiples of the deal.

The problem is that Prestige has seen modest long-term achievements, despite the many deal announcements. Over the past decade, revenues have advanced from $800 million to $1.1 billion, aided by about $1.5 billion in M&A pursued over this period of time, excluding this current transaction. That reveals that, adjusted for acquisitions, organic growth has been difficult to find, certainly if we consider the inflationary impact.

In fact, shares have been flatlining over the past decade, having traded in a $30-$90 range over that period of time. This was a $90 stock in the spring of last year; shares are down a third, driven by the soft operating results lately.

While I am usually attracted to such situations, the reality is that the long-term growth is difficult to find, while leverage is quite high. All this makes me upbeat and curious about this deal. Modest expectations are not enough given a lack of organic growth and high leverage, despite what appears to be a very reasonable deal here.

data.title: Blue Owl Capital: Jamie Dimon's Warning On Private Credit Deserves Attention
data.description:
Over the years, we've reviewed a handful of products in the private credit space and leveraged loans market here on Seeking Alpha, including funds like Oxford Lane (OXLC), Ares Capital (ARCC), and Eagle Point (ECC). We've generally shared our aversion for a lot of these securities in this sector of the market, given the many associated tail risks and fee structures that investors may not realize exist in the search for high-yield "opportunities."

Fast forward to today, and some of the skepticism may have been justified with certain parts of the leveraged loan market, like certain leveraged CLO Equity funds being down 50%, with most publicly traded business development companies, or BDCs (BIZD), down 20%, and most operators having taken an even bigger haircut while the S&P 500 (SP500) remains near its all-time highs.

Given some of the current negative developments in the private credit market, we will assess the current liquidity crisis, triggered by both a crisis of confidence in collateral in the general market and the threat of disruption from AI in the software sector, and examine what this means for the future of Blue Owl Capital Corporation (OBDC).

In addition, we will also evaluate some concerns recently raised by the CEO of JPMorgan for the industry and compare them with OBDC's loan portfolio to explain why we believe the current discount to NAV is largely justified, given the risks currently facing the industry.

A Rising Tide Lifts All Boats

In a recent investor's day call from JPMorgan Chase (JPM), CEO Jamie Dimon issued a grim warning for investors in private credit, signaling his worries about broader complacency in the space, which furthered the selloff in the BDC and leveraged loan space this week, citing that:

Unfortunately, we did see this in 2005 and 2006 and 2007, almost the same thing. The rising tide lifting all boats. Everyone was making a lot of money. People were leveraging to the hilt. The sky was the limit. (Jamie Dimon, JPMorgan Investor Day.)

What's perhaps more pertaining to OBDC were his comments that risks may well be sector specific, unlike previous cycles, broadly placing his focus on the software sector amidst recent disruptions with AI. We personally see this as an issue as well, given that a lot of the loans that have been made from private credit are towards private equity, which has been predominantly investing/ leveraging the software sector post-2008, with software making up 25% of total deal value over the last decade.

For OBDC's sake, while nobody knows exactly how the software disruption in AI will play out, the uncertainty in our view will continue to hamper private equity markets among small and medium-sized software companies. As we see it, liquidity is coming into focus again as serious money is being funneled towards the disruptors like Anthropic (ANTHRO) and OpenAI (OPENAI), with the latter recently raising another $110BN at a $730BN pre-money valuation. On the contrary, while 11.1% of OBDC's lending is in the software sector, management did allude to the fact that LTM revenue and EBITDA growth were 10% and 16% for the category, with LTVs of approx. 30%, meaning we believe this problem to be rather contained for the time being.

Nevertheless, we do still share concerns about how much the recent boom in private credit in general has come at the expense of underwriting standards, as the market has boomed from $250BN in 2008 to $1.7T as of late, during a very favorable credit environment, amidst ZIRP and very few stress-test scenarios. As for the broader private credit industry, Jamie Dimon noted that:

I don't know how long it's going to be great for everybody. I see a couple of people doing some dumb things. They're just doing dumb things to create [net interest income]. (Jamie Dimon, JPMorgan Investor Day.)

A Question Of Liquidity

As for Blue Owl, we're currently seeing liquidity constraints leading to a questioning of collateral, whether justified or not. Earlier this year, one of Blue Owl's private BDCs, OBDC II, was initially set to merge with OBDC, but eventually canceled due to shareholders' discontent about receiving shares in OBDC, which is trading below NAV. Given that some of these products were marketed towards retail investors, who tend to act first and ask questions later, OBDC II had to meet redemption requests and sold off 35% of the portfolio, or $600M of loans in a broader $1.4BN transaction to free up liquidity across 3 funds, including OBDC.

While we believe this was the right thing to do and freed up liquidity, which in OBDC's case can be used to repurchase shares, we're less content with the way management tried to contain concerns about the quality of their collateral. In this instance, while management on the earnings call expressed significant additional demand, Bloomberg later reported that one of the 4 funds the assets were sold to was Kuvare, Blue Owl's affiliated insurance firm, which we believe sent the wrong message to the markets:

However, in our conversations with the small group of buyers that we went out to, we saw very significant additional demand for these assets, well in excess of what we were planning to sell out of OBDC II. (Q4 Earnings Call)

In our view, this deal seems fine, getting confirmation by management that these assets weren't just a fire sale of their best collateral, and it being consistent with their unfunded revolver and DDTL mix at 90% funded and 10% unfunded. In turn, this also dropped the debt to equity ratio from 1.22x to 1.19x, as net assets shrunk from $7.61BN to $7.40BN, but total debt dropped from $9.53BN to $9.30BN. Management also used this opportunity to buy back $148M to buy back shares at 86% of NAV, and has authorized another $300M, which should give investors some temporary reprieve in terms of pressure on NAV for the moment.

In other words, while we're not seeing any serious systemic issues in OBDC’s portfolio at this moment, we are worried about tail-risks that could be involved in what has now become a market-wide issue being a crisis of confidence in private debt collateral. Overall, being in a liquidity constrained environment could end up impacting OBDC in terms of credit spreads, cost of funding, and not being able to issue equity below NAV.

To highlight the current contagion in the broader market, over the last 6 months, we've seen credit issues in First Brands, Tricolor Auto Group, PrimaLend Capital Partners, and, more recently, Market Financial Solutions, amongst others. Most of these companies had issues in terms of underwriting standards, and could lead to further liquidity constraints on private debt markets if this trend continues, with redemptions from non-traded BDCs having exceeded it's 5% limit for Q1.

This issue even made it into the FOMC minutes, which, in addition to highlighting that monitoring is warranted for debt financing in the buildout of AI infrastructure in private credit markets, noted the interconnections with NBFI's poses tail-risks:

Several participants highlighted vulnerabilities associated with the private credit sector and its provision of credit to riskier borrowers, including risks related to interconnections with other types of nonbank financial institutions, such as insurance companies, and banks’ exposure to this sector. (FOMC Minutes.)

As for any liquidity stains showing up for private credit, we believe investors should be monitoring the Bloomberg Senior Loan ETF (BKLN), which is getting closer to the levels signaling a possible credit event this week, while the iShares High Yield Corporate Bond ETF (HYG) remains robust.

A More Challenging Environment

Moving on to OBDC's portfolio, we can see that the portfolio consists of $16.47BN worth of loans, spread across 234 portfolio companies with a weighted average revenue of $1.0BN, EBITDA of $237M, and a debt yield of 10.0%. In terms of portfolio mix, 79% consists of senior secured debt with 73% being first lien debt, followed by 5% second lien, combined with a mix of common/ preferred equity investments, specialty finance investments, and joint ventures that make up the rest.

So in theory, OBDC is well positioned in the capital stack in regard to payment priority in case of defaults, with the annual net loss rate since inception being merely 29bp. Our take in this case is that this is largely attributable to the fact that we haven't had a serious drawn-out credit cycle for over the last 17 years, and that recovery rates are likely to be lower going forward if there's meaningful disruption from AI in blue-collar work. Looking at OBDC's loan book, which we recommend investors look at, the exposure to software and services industries is arguably underestimated comparing management's presentation.

For example, OBDC labels its exposure to software at 11.1%, although the categorization can be quite subjective, when looking at the loan book. One of such examples would be Cornerstone OnDemand, which is defined as human resources on the books with a loan of $153.90M at amortized cost, and is generally defined as a cloud-based development software provider. In addition, BlueSky is defined under business services, and Circana is labeled as an advertising/ media company despite technically being a market research company.

We also find Pluralsight at $44.48M of amortized cost and $48.86M at fair value, which is defined as an education company on the books but is also geared towards software developers/ IT. Pluralsight was written off entirely by Vista Equity in 2024 and recapitalized by the lenders, and has one loan marked at SOFR + 0% and 7.5% PIK, with the second loan marked at SOFR + 3% with 1.5% PIK.

While it's hard to completely quantify OBDC's exposure to sectors that could be disrupted in this fast-paced AI environment, but still having gone through the loan portfolio, we do believe some caution is warranted here.

On a more positive note, looking at the latest Q4 earnings, a large portion of the dividend remains covered with an adjusted NII of $0.36 per share, though the ROE on adjusted NII came down from 12.4% to 9.7%, largely attributable to lower base rates given 96% of the portfolio is floating rate debt. At the same time, OBDC's NAV came down from $15.26 to $14.81, as a result of write-downs, which we believe may continue, given the challenging environment and tighter liquidity conditions for private credit.

Given these write-downs, net income is now $0.23 per share with ROE on net income at 6.3%. With OBDC trading 23% below NAV at the moment, and with a 13.29% yield, we do believe a potential dividend cut is already largely priced in if the underlying loan book doesn't show any serious deterioration going forward. As management alluded to how it thinks about the dividend:

For this quarter, given our strong results, we are maintaining the regular dividend of $0.37. However, we will continue to discuss this carefully with our Board and evaluate the dividend each quarter, particularly as the full effect of these lower rates and spreads are now impacting the portfolio.

We believe investors should also continue to monitor OBDC's non-accrual rate, which is ticking up to 2.7% for 2025 at amortized cost versus 2.2% the prior year. While PIK did come down to 10.3% in FY2025 from 13.2% in the prior year, we argue that this is still relatively high compared to historical precedents in the industry, especially given that SOFR/ debt yields have come down and conditions have become easier on borrowers.

In addition, we see more headwinds when it comes to the cost of funding, with $3.18BN worth of notes coming due before 2030 that was issued in the low-interest-rate environment a couple of years ago at a weighted average interest rate of 3.69%. Nevertheless, we believe OBDC has ample liquidity between now and 2029, with more than $3BN left in its revolving credit facility and $4.2BN if we include undrawn SPV facilities and CLOs. The positive here is that a lot of the CLOs and SPV's have a longer dated maturity based on SOFR, which could reduce some of the funding pressures as SOFR rates have started to come down at the front end of the curve with the Federal Reserve being accommodative.

Finally, after having discussed all of the above tail risks and complexity that comes with lending to the more traditionally riskier borrowers, we also want to address a major reason we're opposed to private credit in general, looking at the fee structures.

In OBDC's case, its portfolio is externally managed by its parent company Blue Owl (OWL), with fees in our view that eat away much of the Alpha that stands to be gained when investing into private credit, considering the level of risk involved. In Q4, total investing income was $447.75M, with $144.63M going to interest expenses, but also $107.98M going to management fees, performance-based incentive fees, and other operating expenses. This means that over 24% of investment income went to management fees in Q4, when a large portion of this fee is based on AUM, and arguably disincentivizes management to mark down loans and incentivizes them to take on PIK and more leverage. We believe this to be an industry-wide issue, though, with OBDC not being the worst offender amongst its peers.

To justify these fees and still pay a 10% dividend yield, OBDC has to take on some leverage, which is manageable, but could backfire in a credit cycle, in which management fees would also magnify losses. OBDC may not be levered at a 10:1 assets/ equity ratio like a traditional bank is, but it still remains levered 2:1 while taking on traditionally riskier borrowers. Over the last 17 years, amidst ZIRP and a broadly generous credit cycle, this has worked out very well for the industry. But below shows an example from OBDC's 10-K, that in good times and net of expenses, a 10% return on its portfolio results in 16.2% returns for shareholders, but -30.3% if the portfolio has a -10% return.

We'd also like to point out that, despite most of OBDC's loan book consisting of first lien senior secured loans, the type of collateral behind these loans matters as well. While private equity holders/ general equity holders are the first ones to take on losses, creditors may still face difficulties recovering value if most of the assets being intangibles, as is often the case in the software/ services industry, and EBITDA growth were to decelerate.

The disruption that AI could pose to most of these SaaS companies also flies in the face to the fact that most were acquired between 2018 and 2025 at ever-higher EV/EBITDA multiples when rates were low, and investors were venturing off into these alternative asset classes. For OBDC, a Net LTV of 42% does appear to be healthy, but in our view can turn very quickly if multiples start to come off multi-year highs and EBITDA were to compress at the same time.

The Bottom Line

For now, OBDC's internal metrics still seem to hold up, notwithstanding the headwinds from lower SOFR spreads that are compressing NII and may lead to a dividend cut with adjusted NII per share dropping below the dividend payout. The market is forward-looking, however, and we do share some concerns about the extent of exposure to companies that could end up seeing disruption from AI in the software/ services sector.

Investors should understand that investing in BDCs comes with risks, including leverage and arguably high fees, which we believe takes away much of the Alpha that stands to be gained. In this sense, we see private credit and BDCs continuing to underperform the S&P 500 (SPY), and believe investors who are looking for income to be better off investing in a mix of T-bills and a stable index like the S&P 500 equal weight (RSP) or the Schwab US Dividend ETF (SCHD).

In this case, the companies in these indexes are more mature and exposed to less leverage/tail risks, have a relatively low Beta, and a higher return while having the same or lower drawdown profiles in the case of a recession or liquidity shock.

As for OBDC, we believe the BDC is currently fairly valued considering the risks, and its valuation at 0.76x P/NAV compared to the broader market, which is also pricing in a 25% haircut amidst tougher conditions and more uncertainty in the software/ services sector and liquidity concerns in the private credit market in general. Thus, we rate OBDC as a hold, in line with Seeking Alpha's Quant, which cites Growth, Momentum, and Revisions as areas for improvement.

data.title: The 'Monetary Truman Show' Is Over: The Fed Is No Longer In Control
data.description:
The Truman Show

Can you recall the ending shot of The Truman Show? That is where Truman, an individual living in a transparent dome with the belief that the sky above him is real and his neighbors are his friends, sets sail at the end of his world. When his boat hits a painted blue wall at the edge of the dome, the illusion vanishes. On Wednesday evening in Washington, D.C., Jerome Powell was doing the same with the global economy. Before entering the room filled with journalist colleagues and expecting the common "we will wait and see," Jerome Powell pulled out the gray file containing all information and said something that I didn't expect. He said something to the effect that this was not what I expected, as the implications are chilling: "We just don't know." I'm not sure if this is clear, however, when arguably the most influential figure in monetary policy (I believe the Fed has more of an impact on monetary policy than any other entity) states that he does not have a clue or that he can't find his way, the implications are significant.

Less certainty, more data

The market has functioned with an orderliness of logic for many years. Much like the following: falling inflation means less restrictive central banks, therefore falling rates, and therefore expanding multiples.

This orderly logic (imperfect as it may be) has provided investors with their model of action for well more than ten years, if not longer.

The orderly logic described above is beginning to crack today. At the last FOMC meeting, Chairman Powell essentially gave a strong signal to the market, namely, confirmation that the current state of the economy does not lend itself to being interpreted as a linear relationship.

Forget the words, but think about the concept. The Federal Reserve faces an uncharted and very complicated situation. While inflation has slowed down, it still remains at risk from the impacts of energy price shock. The labor market continues to function well but is beginning to normalize.

Economic growth is being maintained within a geopolitical environment that will continue to present volatility. In addition to these factors, we have the emergence of a new factor - the possible consequences of the impact of artificial intelligence on productivity, job creation, and income inequality.

As such, the typical monetary policy framework is less effective as a predictor in this type of economic environment. Asset prices often become more erratic.

The implications for bonds, equities, and currencies/commodities are cross-cutting. In terms of bonds, this means that investors will be increasingly uncertain regarding the direction of interest rates (particularly short-term rates) than they would be under a standard regime. For equities, it implies that multiples can expand in a less predictable fashion and are now largely contingent upon new data. External or exogenous shocks to the system become more visible for currencies and commodities given specific emphasis on geopolitical events and oil prices.

However, there is also a 'harder' or more 'reality-based' aspect to this issue. If investors are unable to predict how the Fed will react to changing economic conditions, they will also be unable to predict the cost of borrowing money, the overall state of financial conditions, and therefore, ultimately, the decisions made by households and businesses.

We are now going from a "managed" or "guided" economic cycle to a more free-flowing one where markets will pay considerably more attention to data than the narratives told about it. Consequently, we will progressively less use past certainty in our decision-making process.

As an investor, you will need to adjust your thinking about how you invest. You will rely less on pre-defined models and focus more on the real-time dynamics of the data.

Dot Plot

The central theme of the meeting was the dot plot. This is the chart the Fed provides to show where individual members of the committee believe interest rates and inflation will be over the next few years.

It is not a commitment or even an expectation of future action, but rather a reflection of their current views on the economic environment. In December, the expectations were relatively clear: that inflation would move back toward the Fed’s stated target of 2%. The Personal Consumption Expenditure (PCE) index was estimated to be 2.4 percent in 2026. Today this is no longer the case. The revised estimate places the inflation rate at 2.7 percent. The same is true for the core component of inflation, which is watched most closely by the Fed because it does not include the volatile components of food and energy.

It’s not an enormous difference in terms of quantity, but it is a large change in terms of direction. Also, the forecast for economic growth has been revised marginally upward. Thus, the image emerging from this is that the economy is doing fine, but inflation is finding it hard to fall.

In spite of this, the Federal Reserve is still signaling a possibility of interest rate cuts in the next few years. This is where the message becomes somewhat muddled. Since the U.S. economy is experiencing good growth and above-target inflation, there appears to be limited room for interest rate cuts as opposed to the opposite.

The market reacted almost instantaneously to this. Short-term yields—particularly two-year yields—increased substantially in both the U.S. and Europe. A fairly definitive sign: diminished confidence in a rapid easing of monetary policy. In essence, the reaction was a revision of expectations regarding future changes to the level of interest rates and, most importantly, how easy it will be to revert back to an easier interest rate environment.

Banking Stimulus Overheating the Economy

There is one other factor that is often overlooked — however it is very important. Powell may be cautious with regard to monetary policy, but on the regulatory side, things are being done exactly the opposite. Major American banks will have access to $200 billion in additional capital to invest. By the end of this week, U.S. regulators will publish new proposals outlining how they intend to modify, or even soften, capital regulation rules — and what this means is that banks will have the ability to do more buyback programs, lend more money and trade more. However, there is a risk involved. Excessive speed in releasing all of this liquidity could harmfully overheat both the overall economy as well as the housing market.

Prior to now, there were many discussions surrounding more restrictive requirements (Michael Barr's proposal was for an increase in reserve requirements), and thus banks had also held back from committing capital to their respective balance sheets pending clarity on what the regulatory requirements would be.

As a result of the softer stance toward the regulatory requirements advocated by Michelle Bowman, the capital that previously was unavailable to banks is once again available.

Of course, the largest beneficiary will be the major banks. For example, as a result of having such a large capital surplus prior to these rule changes, JPMorgan is likely to have even greater margins of capital surplus as a result of the changes. Similar to varying degrees, this may also apply to Bank of America, Wells Fargo, Goldman Sachs, Citigroup, and Morgan Stanley.

Where will the money go?

Now that the changes have been implemented, the obvious question is, where will the money go? I see just three options:

  1. A portion of it can be distributed to shareholders by way of share repurchases. However, it seems fairly apparent at this juncture that there is a ceiling on this as well. Share repurchases become inefficient when stock prices are already so high, relative to their past levels.
  2. Another option is an increased level of trading activity, which will generate additional market exposure.
  3. But the key remaining leverage is credit. Additional available capital from this regulatory change could lead to additional lending to both households and businesses.

Is this a form of a "monetary buyback"? Is this a "disguised" form of quantitative easing? Call it whatever you want. This is the same idea. On the one hand, the Fed is continuing to hold interest rates at very high levels, in an effort to slow down the economy. On the other hand, this regulatory change may provide the banks with another source of stimulus through the banking system itself.

Who Wins?
