Personally, I think this is a gift for the big banks, and for the American economy.

If we are looking at the banks, I think JPMorgan (JPM) has the best position. JPMorgan already has around $60 billion in excess capital, and with less restrictive regulations, it can quickly get to $70–$75 billion. That is an enormous amount of money, and it puts JPMorgan well ahead of the rest of the banking system.

It’s not just JPM, however. Bank of America (BOFA), Wells Fargo (WFC), Goldman Sachs (GS), Citigroup (C), and Morgan Stanley (MS) all also have somewhere between $20–$30 billion in excess capital. When you add them all together, it becomes apparent where that $200 billion we’re talking about came from.

The real issue here is “what are they going to do with it?” As I mentioned earlier, the answer is credit. If you allow banks to use their excess capital, at some point in time, that capital will make its way into the real economy. With more credit available, there will be more borrowing, more leverage, and more economic activity. And this is where the interesting — albeit somewhat contradictory part of this story comes from. The Federal Reserve has interest rates high to slow down the economy. At the same time, the Federal Reserve is creating conditions for the banking system to once again start growing.

A New World

We have moved into a new world, beyond the artificial world of the "Monetary Truman Show". When Powell stated, with clarity, that the Fed "does not know" what would occur going forward, for me all is changed.

The variables that remain constant:

  1. Inflation/Slowing Economy: We are no longer simply experiencing a short-term slowdown in the economy. As the core inflation rate exceeds 2.7 percent annually and continues to increase along with economic growth, there can no longer be a reasonable argument for reducing interest rates in the near future.
  2. Structural Energy Disruption: A major portion of Qatar's LNG production was damaged by the attack on its facilities. Therefore, while recovery will likely take years rather than months; as such, high-cost energy prices will likely prevail for an extended period. As a result, we will see subsequent increases in inflation, as well as potential decreases in production and/or availability of goods through our supply chain.
  3. Chaos of Fed Leadership: The transition from Powell to Warsh is not linear. The Fed leadership transition has evolved to include a political element. Warsh is betting on the arrival of AI driven productivity to allow for a more accommodative Fed. However, should that productivity fail to arrive at the correct time, there is a high probability that inflation will once again begin to rise.
  4. Asset Allocation: We are witnessing a trend that is typical of periods of stress. Leverage is being reduced, and even the most successful assets are being sold. That is why gold and equities may both decline today. It is not contradictory. Rather, it represents the movement of liquidity. I will also include an additional element that some of my recent client inquiries have centered on: Why is gold dropping? Didn't gold represent the uncorrelated asset? The safe haven asset? Today's gold is far more financialized than gold was in years past. Today there are a large number of leveraged investors. When a market declines, it is necessary to pay off margin calls. You will sell whatever you can to meet your margin call obligations. Frequently you will sell the very investment that had performed well. Gold becomes the first ATM.

Liquidity, discipline, and understanding the scenario

The last few weeks, as I noted in my previous reports, my approach has been to have high levels of liquidity (and discipline). At this point in time, the most important thing is to manage risk rather than try to find the perfect entry. Traditional buy-and-hold investors will continue to do nothing. Flexible investors (such as myself) will have liquidity in our portfolios and we will utilize it when the situation is right for us.

If the P/E ratio for the indexes falls, it does not mean this is a disaster. This is part of a normal correction after many years of growth; as well as a healthy process. The true issue is another one; the market is trying to figure out what geopolitical reality we live in. If Trump says he is calling the game in the next couple of weeks, however, Iran continues to put pressure on the Strait of Hormuz, little has changed. The market does not respond to declarations; it responds to actual energy flows. Personally, if the situation escalates, I believe an additional 5-6% decline in the S&P 500 from current levels is possible. The main area to watch would be around 6,250 points. The market watches and reacts to these areas as they are closely watched technical and psychological areas (it’s no accident that the volume of news increases just when we reach these areas).

However, right now, I do not think anyone can estimate the exact amount of decline. All of this will depend on the direction of the conflict. Therefore, the best way to continue to invest is to use scenarios and adjust your investment exposure.

What is the maximum price of energy? I have three different scenarios in mind:

  1. A “quick exit” scenario: Oil around $65.00
  2. An intermediate scenario: Peak at $110.00 and eventually fall back to $80.00
  3. A severe scenario (the strait blocked): Prices could potentially rise to $170.

data.title: Handling The Current War-Related Volatility
data.description:
Over the past few weeks, I've seen an influx of requests on coverage for companies that see either a positive or a negative correlation from the ongoing conflict. While I personally believe that I, at this stage, am well-prepared in any event to handle the volatility that's going on (be it up or downside) I can understand how some investors, especially if you do trading, may be rattled by the swift movements that are currently characteristic of the market.

There will be advice or input given from the left and right on how you should be acting, whether to buy or sell, and whether to act this way or that. It's important to know what you're doing in these events. The reason for this is that if youdo not have a firm handle on what you're doing, this is likely to increase your anxiety, which in turn increases your likelihood of making emotionally driven investment decisions.

Emotionally driven investment decisions are things to always avoid.

In this article, I will be sharing with my readership (and Seeking Alpha followers), what characterizes my current market actions and how I think about my portfolio, what I cover, and how I allocate. If you know that I'm an IG leader as well as an asset manager, you also know that this not only influences me, but what I manage, and perhaps those that follow me.

Handling Volatility - diversification, panic, and emotion

How you handle your investing during a volatile period largely depends on how you are invested during that time. I will use myself as an example. As a value-oriented investor, my primary motivation and strategy is identifying cheap, selling expensive, and ignoring the market as a whole (in terms of momentary trends and ups and downs).

I've been witness to over 5 significant periods of volatility during my career. In all periods, except this one, I was invested over 97%, with less than 3% cash. This time around, I was invested at only 55% with over 40% cash. The reason for this was simple. For a few months prior to the Iran war, this time's volatility event, I viewed the market as significantly overvalued. This led me to rotate significant parts of my profitable investments. The only investments I did not rotate, which I have rotated now during the volatility period, were those related to oil and energy. As of a short time ago, I have rotated all of my investments in this sector at significant amounts of profits - as a result I am now almost 50% cash, the highest I have been since I started investing many years ago.

This obviously doesn't mean that I'm uninvested. Most of the stocks I still hold are quality companies where I expect significant upside, even if that upside and the timing of it have become more uncertain since the war.

The hardest part for me to learn (and what I am still learning) is combining my long-term mindset/tolerance with a short-term openness for rotation/purchases when needed. This is something that requires maturity and calm from the investor.

Maturity and calm, in my view, can only exist if you're not worried about your investments or their short-term. Thus, if you're invested in a way that threatens your personal, financial well-being if your investments don't work out, you're far more likely to make emotionally-driven investment decisions.

This is why I tend to work with only, and why my IG is aimed at intermediate and above type investors. The type of investors who know their goals, know their finances, and are not liable to be spooked by short-term movements. It's the type of investor that I would argue you should be.

A good way to become such an investor is to ensure that all of your personal needs and costs are met by capital or streams of capital outside of, and separate from your investment account. I.e, the notion of your investment account being your "only income", is something I consider possible only if your account is large or diversified enough, or if you have assets/streams of income outside that generate enough for your personal well-being.

I use a combination of the two. I have substantial income from dividends, even at only 50% invested, but I also have substantial cash flow from outside my investments.

This is a question of strategic approach. If you haven't yet thought in these terms, then I encourage you to sit down and, in detail, plan this. A core question that I typically ask, to begin with, is how much capital someone needs to feel, and to be safe for a 3-year period. Having this capital secured either as cash flow or as cash is usually a good starting point. If you on the other hand (hypothetically) say that your capital needs are met because you invest a $100k portfolio in 15%-yielding junk bonds coupled with a $2,500 freelancer income (per month), then I would argue you are very, very far from having your needs met. (Extreme example, but one i've heard).

My mantra is long-term goals. Long-term goals are not incompatible with short-term approach or goals or approaches, but planning is needed. Portfolio moves should be considered with care, and with the impact they have on your portfolio.

Ideally, you should have an Excel sheet, a notebook, a piece of paper (whatever you're comfortable with), detailing your incoming and outgoing cash and capital, not just for the next month, but for as long as you feel secure forecasting.

This, in my mind, is the core and key to staying invested, staying calm, and not being rocked by crises. It's why during the worst days of the COVID-19 crash, when the markets were rocking double-digits, I was able to ignore noise and allocate capital. If I hadn't had what I describe above, it's likely I would have been open to emotional investing - and that is akin to being a piece of bark in a whirlpool. Not a situation you ever want to find yourself in.

Choosing your targets and understanding the market volatility

There are arguments on both sides of this. Some investors will argue that high volatility is the wrong period to invest, and it's better to wait for periods of calm - others will say it's the perfect time to invest.

I will take the middle ground. I say that war volatility isn't the time to invest extremely high amounts of capital. This is due to the risk of severe underperformance and capital loss. This, by the way, goes not for crashes, but for inflated balloons, as we are currently seeing in oil and energy, for instance. The nature of the whipsaw markets creates sudden swings, and panic pricing, especially on the high side, has historically resulted in flat or negative developments for over 10 years. Some who invested during the GFC (before the crash) were still not positive by 2024.

Those who argue that a war is the best time to invest argue, indirectly, that they can either predict where it's going or that these events follow typical cycles. They do not. Cycle A is not Cycle B.

While it is true that markets recover from all (so far) shocks, this usually only happens after downward pressure. Ergo, if you haven't seen downward pressure, and things are still swinging up, it's probably too early to invest substantially.

This is why I prefer holding capital during the swings of a crisis, maintain my excessive diversification during that crisis, and deploy only once I see either signals of capitulation or calm - and usually not in big chunks, but rather small, carefully considered "packages". I also "prune" positions that in that specific crisis - oil and energy now, for instance - are liable to grow more volatile, even if there's significant potential for upside.

This echoes a strategy I also apply to asset management. Usually, careful investors with substantial capital understand or even teach that it's better to be safe than sorry. Building wealth takes immense time and work - losing it can be done very quickly. It's why, usually, the more wealth you amass, the more conservative you become.

It's why it makes me grimace when I see a new investor, who has just saved his first $10k, do extremely high-risk investments in the hope of multiplying that by 5-10x.

Protect before you attack - that's my approach. I'd rather take a 25% profit than the potential for a 75% profit, but at a substantially higher degree of risk.

You may personally disagree with this - and this is fine. Many investors I know do it differently. That is why there are asset managers and approaches for both customer A and B. I don't pretend to be an AI-focused or IT-focused investor. I know my lane, and I stick to it, for the most part.

If what I am writing here resonates with you, and you find yourself either agreeing or disagreeing strongly, then chances are you have an investment strategy that works for you - and I applaud this. The danger that I see is that many investors and even analysts say "This investment/portfolio works for everyone". I've yet to see that for any single portfolio. Many analysts argue that this is their forever portfolio. Such as "Holding this stock/portfolio forever." Then that portfolio fails, and they'll find the next set of stocks, funds, or investments they believe will set them up forever.

Don't get me wrong - I used to make this mistake too. The truth is (according to me), there are no investments you can hold forever and hope to be profitable. The mere notion of any analyst arguing that any company is a "forever" hold is completely contrary to how capitalism, the business disruption lifecycle, and market changes work. Right now, many may argue that Google (GOOG) will never go anywhere, or Amazon (AMZN). What do you think people 40 years ago would say about Eastman Kodak, Sears, GM, a bank like Continental Illinois, or IBM? Probably the same investors say about these other companies today.

It's my view that as a market participant, you need to be flexible and open to new understandings and realities.

We don't know how long the current conflict is going to last. It could end next week - it could go on for another year. Like any situation in the market, the important thing is to keep your eyes on your reality and goals, not the goals and targets others say that you should have. The current market trends are driving some sectors to the peaks, while other sectors are in free-fall.

A good piece of advice to anyone in a crisis such as this is to not to overconsume news, because this may lead to emotionally driven investment decisions.

What I do in events like these (companies and sectors)

Wars tend to drive inflation. We're already seeing this. This is contrary to the current US desire to lower interest rates, because during times of war, central banks typically raise them. This, in turn, can affect valuations for companies, especially in gas, food, and oil. There is also a risk in holding war-affected FX, but I doubt anyone is holding Iranian rials.

Identified sectors in free fall a this time are commercial shipping (but as we've seen in some coverage, this has actually caused some of these companies to experience not only surges in freight, but also insurance), middle eastern stock markets (and others), export industries (LNG and oil, the effects of the force majeure declared will have to be seen).

At this point, while there are avenues for investing in the war - classic ones include energy, defense companies, and safe-haven commodity assets (albeit non-productive ones) like gold, my bet is currently on keeping my "cash stash" in treasuries, interest accounts, and key undervalued equities in sectors regardless of the war. The war hasn't stopped making these companies undervalued. In some cases, they have even made them more so. That is why the war doesn't make me change my approach - it, in fact, enhances it (because the companies tend to drop more). I also offer the opportunity to find undervalued gems elsewhere.

So, the rationale that I use is to watch the market with interest, but neither fear nor concern. All of my investments are thoroughly vetted. A dividend cut may change a target, but very rarely the entire appeal of the stock. At times, it can even denote even more appeal.

It's my view that many investors and many market participants can gain insight and a sense of calm by doing what I am describing in this article.

War, while horrible in its own way, problematic for investors, and definitely an unstable factor, certainly brings with it challenges - but as many investors have also described, opportunities.

What I have given you here is an insight into my mind and approach - one I hope to be helpful to you. This is regardless if you view the market as I do, if you agree, or if you disagree.

My main point to any investor is always to know your investment strategy, and proudly stick to it provided it fulfills your goals.

The only concern for you should be if you encounter a situation and you find yourself and your strategy, portfolio, or economy unprepared. That's where i'd argue you could probably gain from going back to the basics.

And that's what I'm here for.
